canonical_definition: Across the surveyed literature, MATH is a prevalent benchmark used to evaluate advanced mathematical reasoning in large language models. It is consistently defined as a dataset of challenging, competition-level high school mathematics problems, frequently citing sources like the American Mathematics Competitions (AMC) and the American Invitational Mathematics Examination (AIME). The dataset is described as containing 12,500 problems spanning subjects such as algebra, geometry, and number theory, often categorized by difficulty and including step-by-step solutions with boxed answers to facilitate automated evaluation. The overwhelming majority of papers use MATH to measure `Mathematical reasoning`, often alongside GSM8K, with MATH being positioned as more difficult; as one paper states, it is "significantly more challenging than GSM8K". A smaller body of work also employs MATH to assess other constructs, including `Generalization`, specifically for "easy-to-hard generalization," as well as `Complex reasoning` and `In-context learning`. While most commonly referred to as MATH, it is sometimes called "Hendrycks-MATH," and researchers have created modified versions like "MATH*" for specific experiments or integrated it into composite datasets such as "MATH-Combined". It is also distinguished from a different benchmark named the "Mathematics Dataset" from DeepMind. Evaluation is typically performed using metrics like exact match or Pass@1 on the final answer.

definitions.text: A dataset of challenging high school mathematical competition problems from sources like AMC 10, AMC 12, and AIME, used to evaluate advanced mathematical reasoning.
definitions.paper: Are Human-generated Demonstrations Necessary for In-context Learning?

definitions.text: A mathematical problem-solving benchmark used to evaluate math performance after fine-tuning.
definitions.paper: CorDA: Context-Oriented Decomposition Adaptation of Large Language Models for Task-Aware Parameter-Efficient Fine-tuning

definitions.text: A modified version of the MATH dataset created for on-policy DPO experiments, where preference pairs are generated by selecting the best and worst responses from a policy model's own outputs.
definitions.paper: 3D-Properties: Identifying Challenges in DPO and Charting a Path Forward

definitions.text: A composite dataset created for this paper by integrating several existing mathematical reasoning benchmarks (GSM8k, MathQA, SVAMP, MATH) into a uniform format.

MathGAP (Mathematical Generalization on Arithmetic Proofs), a synthetic evaluation framework that generates arithmetic word problems and chain-of-thought solutions with controllable proof-tree complexity for out-of-distribution testing.
definitions.paper: MathGAP: Out-of-Distribution Evaluation on Problems with Arbitrarily Complex Proofs

definitions.text: A benchmark for evaluating mathematical reasoning by testing models on problems from the MATH dataset.
definitions.paper: COAT: Compressing Optimizer states and Activations for Memory-Efficient FP8 Training

definitions.text: Hendrycks-MATH is a mathematical reasoning benchmark covering competition-style math problems.
definitions.paper: Mixture of Parrots: Experts improve memorization more than reasoning

definitions.text: A dataset of mathematical problems covering areas such as algebra, calculus, and probability, generated by a curriculum-based process.
definitions.paper: Mini-batch Coresets for Memory-efficient Language Model Training on Data Mixtures

definitions.text: A dataset from DeepMind for evaluating mathematical reasoning, covering topics such as algebra, arithmetic, calculus, and probability.

relationships.paper: Don't Trust: Verify -- Grounding LLM Quantitative Reasoning with Autoformalization
relationships.evidence: We evaluate DTV on three quantitative reasoning datasets: GSM8K (Cobbe et al., 2021), MATH (Hendrycks et al., 2021) and MultiArith (Roy & Roth, 2016).

characterization: Across the surveyed literature, MATH is a prevalent benchmark used to evaluate advanced mathematical reasoning in large language models. It is consistently defined as a dataset of challenging, competition-level high school mathematics problems, frequently citing sources like the American Mathematics Competitions (AMC) and the American Invitational Mathematics Examination (AIME). The dataset is described as containing 12,500 problems spanning subjects such as algebra, geometry, and number theory, often categorized by difficulty and including step-by-step solutions with boxed answers to facilitate automated evaluation. The overwhelming majority of papers use MATH to measure `Mathematical reasoning`, often alongside GSM8K, with MATH being positioned as more difficult; as one paper states, it is "significantly more challenging than GSM8K". A smaller body of work also employs MATH to assess other constructs, including `Generalization`, specifically for "easy-to-hard generalization," as well as `Complex reasoning` and `In-context learning`. While most commonly referred to as MATH, it is sometimes called "Hendrycks-MATH," and researchers have created modified versions like "MATH*" for specific experiments or integrated it into composite datasets such as "MATH-Combined". It is also distinguished from a different benchmark named the "Mathematics Dataset" from DeepMind. Evaluation is typically performed using metrics like exact match or Pass@1 on the final answer.
